title: Buatsi vs Yarde: Britain's light-heavyweight reckoning, and a clash of methods
summary: One has a 27-fight knockout habit and a record that swings. The other has lost just once and tends to win on the cards. British light-heavyweight has waited a while for this.
body:
British light-heavyweight has waited a long time to put Joshua Buatsi and Anthony Yarde in the same ring. The appeal is in the mismatch of methods. These are two good fighters who arrive at the same place by opposite routes.

Yarde: the finisher who lives dangerously

Yarde's 27-4 record is built on power. He has stopped 24 opponents, an 89 per cent knockout rate that places him among the heavier hitters in the division. The cost of that style sits right next to it: four defeats, and a recent five that opens with a knockout loss. When Yarde is in a fight, it tends to be a real one, settled by who lands rather than who out-points.

Buatsi: quieter, and harder to catch

Buatsi wins with less drama. His 20-1 record includes 13 knockouts at a 65 per cent clip but also seven decisions, and his recent form is a steady accumulation of points wins rather than highlight finishes. He is the bigger man too, 6ft 2in to Yarde's 6ft, with a two-inch reach advantage to keep range with.

Power against control

This is a puncher with one clear defeat against a boxer with one clear defeat. It turns on whether Yarde's power finds the chin before Buatsi's length and control quietly bank the rounds. The records say Yarde is the more likely to end it early, and also the more likely to be ended.
